coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon dots/g-C3N4 photoexcitation simultaneously enhanced the microbial degradation of nitrate and oxytetracycline in a low C/N ratio aquaculture wastewater
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Pin
Chen., Lixiang
Yan, Xinyu
Bai, Rui
Adyari, Bob
Zhou, Xudong
Zhao, Feng
coredata.dc:description: The low carbon-to-nitrogen (C/N) ratio in aquaculture wastewater presents challenges for biological treatments, particularly regarding combined pollution, such as nitrate and oxytetracycline. Herein, this study introduces a synergistic system that integrates photocatalysts (N-doped carbon dots/g-C3N4) with a domesticated microbial community. In artificial wastewater at a C/N ratio of 2, the synergistic system removed completely nitrate and degraded 91.2 % of oxytetracycline simultaneously at 24 h. Moreover, within this system, the accumulation of nitrite was effectively eliminated and the nitrogen production ratio was increased to 82.6 %. Noticeably, oxytetracycline was transformed efficiently into low molecular weight products. In the synergistic treatment process, photocatalysis serves a role in enhancing biodegradation. The generated photoelectrons are efficiently transported through the biofilms, boosting microbial metabolic activity. This increase in microbial activity leads to improved removal of both nitrate and oxytetracycline. The synergistic system was also operated in actual aquaculture wastewater environment to evaluate its practical application potential. The Synergistic system exhibited removal efficiencies for nitrate and oxytetracycline that were 2.1 and 1.8 times higher, respectively, compared to biodegradation treatments. The proposed synergistic system offers a promising strategy to simultaneously tackle nitrate-oxytetracycline pollution, particularly in wastewater characterized by a low C/N ratio, without limiting its applicability to aquaculture wastewater.

Effect of different treatments on the concentration of denitrification process intermediates, including nitrite (a), nitrous oxide (b), ammonia (c), and nitrogen gas production ratio (d).

Intermediate products of oxytetracycline treated by biodegradation and synergistic degradation (a), Fathead minnow LC50 (b) and developmental toxicity of oxytetracycline and intermediate products treated by photocatalysis (c).

Photo-electrochemical characterization of photocatalytic and synergistic system: Current vs time applied with no bias (a), Linear scan voltammogram tests ranged from 0.05-0.80 V at a scan rate of 10 mV/s (b), Photo-electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of each test with no bias, the frequency was changed from 10 Hz to 200 kHz (c), ETSA test in biofilm from biodegradation treatment and synergistic treatment (d).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The low carbon-to-nitrogen (C/N) ratio in aquaculture wastewater presents challenges for biological treatments, particularly regarding combined pollution, such as nitrate and oxytetracycline. Herein, this study introduces a synergistic system that integrates photocatalysts (N-doped carbon dots/g-C3N4) with a domesticated microbial community. In artificial wastewater at a C/N ratio of 2, the synergistic system removed completely nitrate and degraded 91.2 % of oxytetracycline simultaneously at 24 h. Moreover, within this system, the accumulation of nitrite was effectively eliminated and the nitrogen production ratio was increased to 82.6 %. Noticeably, oxytetracycline was transformed efficiently into low molecular weight products. In the synergistic treatment process, photocatalysis serves a role in enhancing biodegradation. The generated photoelectrons are efficiently transported through the biofilms, boosting microbial metabolic activity. This increase in microbial activity leads to improved removal of both nitrate and oxytetracycline. The synergistic system was also operated in actual aquaculture wastewater environment to evaluate its practical application potential. The Synergistic system exhibited removal efficiencies for nitrate and oxytetracycline that were 2.1 and 1.8 times higher, respectively, compared to biodegradation treatments. The proposed synergistic system offers a promising strategy to simultaneously tackle nitrate-oxytetracycline pollution, particularly in wastewater characterized by a low C/N ratio, without limiting its applicability to aquaculture wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The world’s annual fish production exceeded 80 million tons [1], resulting in a substantial generation of aquaculture wastewater, particularly from industrial recirculating aquaculture systems. These systems frequently have effluents with high concentrations of nitrate and antibiotics. Nitrate concentrations were reported to reach 200 mg/L [2], leading to eutrophication [3]. Oxytetracycline, a common antibiotic in aquaculture wastewater, typically has a concentration ranged from 0.24 to 15.1 mg/ L [4,5]. Antibiotics could accumulate within the food web, thereby contributing to the occurrence of antibiotic-resistant bacteria, and affecting human health. Concurrently, various pollutants coexist in the environment, may have synergistic effects. For instance, residual nitrogenous compounds such as nitrate and ammonia have been reported to promote the horizontal transfer of antibiotic-resistance genes, exacerbating the ecological risk posed by antibiotics [6,7].

Biological treatment has been employed to remove the combined pollutants of nitrate and antibiotics. For example, sulfur-reducing bacteria were able to remove nitrate and oxytetracycline simultaneously with a C/N ratio of 4.5, attaining 98.8 % nitrate removal and 76.1 % tetracycline removal within 72 h [8]. In another experiment, an up-flow immobilized biofilter was utilized for simultaneous nitrate and tetracycline removal, achieving complete denitrification and 67.8 % tetracycline removal with a C/N ratio of 6 [9]. Additionally, Urobacterium sp. KSS10 was observed to remove 63.3 % tetracycline and 98.6 % nitrate within 96 h, using a relatively high organic carbon source concentration of 5 g/L [10]. However, aquaculture wastewater commonly presents a low C/N ratio, approximately 3–4 [11]. This low ratio limits the availability of carbon sources, thereby constraining the supply of electrons necessary for microbial denitrification [12,13]. Besides, antibiotics are typically removed through microbial co-metabolism with other substrates [14]. Limited carbon sources can hinder microbial growth and metabolism, reducing antibiotic degradation efficiency [15]. In summary, a low C/N ratio in aquaculture wastewater has been found to hinder the efficiency of biological treatments in addressing nitrate and antibiotic pollution [16]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop technologies for better microbial nitrate-antibiotic pollution removal in aquaculture wastewater.

Microorganisms can harness electrons generated on the surfaces of semiconductors or solid electrodes to boost their growth and metabolic processes [17]. This capacity plays a pivotal role in pollutant biodegradation and offers valuable insights into the synergistic photocatalysis-biodegradation systems. Zhu et al employed an innovative bacterial-algal biofilm-TiO2 synergistic system to remove 95.2 % of nitrate. In this system, the photocatalyst served as the electron donor pool, allowing for efficient nitrate reduction [18]. In addition, a 40.3 % nitrate reduction within 16 h was achieved in the TiO2/C3N4-microorganism synergistic system [19]. Furthermore, the synergistic system also demonstrated efficacy in the elimination of antibiotics. Ding et al applied visible light to excite Bi12O17Cl2, leading to the modulation of the microbial community and resulting in complete oxytetracycline removal within 2 h [20]. In a separate study, a C3N4-microbes synergistic system possessed the capability to remove 94 % of ciprofloxacin within 24 h [21]. However, most of these synergistic systems have predominantly addressed a singular contaminant, focusing on, either nitrate or antibiotic. Given the coexistence of nitrate and oxytetracycline in aquaculture wastewater and the consequential environmental concerns, there is an urgent need to develop techniques capable of simultaneously eliminating these combined pollutants.

This study establishes a synergistic system integrating N-doped carbon dots/graphitic carbon nitride (NCDS/g-C3N4) and domesticated microbial community for the concurrent removal of nitrate and oxytetracycline contamination, with the main objectives outlined as follows: (1) demonstrate the enhancement of simultaneous biodegradation of nitrate and oxytetracycline through the synergistic system, (2) investigate the mechanism of photocatalysis influencing the biodegradation of nitrate and oxytetracycline, (3) assess the system’s application potential in the treatment of actual aquaculture wastewater.

Analytical grade sodium nitrate (NaNO3) was procured from Sinopharm Group Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and oxytetracycline was sourced from Dr. Ehrenstorfer GmbH (Augsburg, Germany). Formic acid and methanol were purchased from Merck KGaA (Darmstadt, Germany). Other chemicals and reagents used in the culture medium (refer to Tables S1-S3) were of analytical grade and purchased from Sinopharm Group Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All solutions were prepared in ultrapure water throughout the experiment.

The polyurethane sponge cubes used in this study were of dimensions 5 mm × 5 mm × 5 mm with a porosity of 1.5 m2/g (Jiangsu Aiqin Environmental Technology Co., Ltd), and N-doped carbon dots/g-C3N4 (NCDS/g-C3N4) served as photocatalyst. The detailed preparation method and characterization results were described in supplementary information Method 1 and Fig. S1-S3.

The assembly process for the photocatalyst and polyurethane sponge was conducted as follows: 1.0 g of NCDS/g-C3N4 powder and 1.0 g of uncoated sponge carrier cubes were ultrasonically dispersed in 100.0 mL of anhydrous ethanol for 30 min. Subsequently, the above suspension was heated to 60 °C and stirred until all the liquid evaporated. Finally, the uncoated NCDS/g-C3N4 powder was removed by sonicating for 1 min and rinsed three times with deionized water, then dried at 60 °C.

Wastewater effluent was collected from a farm and subjected to cultivation with oxytetracycline and nitrate for approximately ten months to simulate the proliferation of microbial community. Subsequently, the acclimated microbes were separated by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 10 min. For cultivation, the NCDS/g-C3N4 coated carriers and collected microorganisms were mixed in a culture solution (Tables S1-S3). The cultivation duration spanned six days, with the solution being refreshed every two days.

A 300 W tungsten halogen lamp (Philips, The Netherlands) with a wavelength range spanning from 380 nm to 800 nm, equipped with a filter was used as a visible light source. The light intensity was set at 100 mW/cm2. Experiments were carried out for 24 h using a circulating water system to prevent thermal catalytic effects. For control purposes, separate treatments for biodegradation, photocatalysis, absorption, and photolysis were established. For biodegradation treatment, 0.5 g NCDS/g-C3N4 coated polyurethane sponge was used for microbial cultivation six days before the experiment. In photocatalysis treatment, 0.5 g NCDS/g-C3N4 polyurethane sponge was used for the combined removal of pollutants under visible light irradiation, without the need for microbial cultivation. Absorption treatment was also conducted using 0.5 g NCDS/g-C3N4 coated polyurethane sponge under dark conditions without microbial cultivation. The photolysis experiment was carried out using the same light source as in the photocatalysis treatment on 0.5 g raw polyurethane sponge, without microbial cultivation. For the operation of “synergistic degradation treatment,” after cultivating microorganisms on the 0.5 g NCDS/g-C3N4 coated polyurethane sponge, visible light was used for photoexcitation and removal of combined pollutants. Each experiment was conducted in a 150 mL beaker containing 75 mL of aquaculture wastewater. The initial concentration of the nitrate and oxytetracycline were 100.0 mg/L and 4.0 mg/L, respectively. The C/N ratio was set at 2/1 by adding methanol. Actual aquaculture wastewater was obtained from Yang Shengwen Aquaculture Farm in Xiamen, China (Table S4). The experimental operating conditions for the actual aquaculture wastewater were maintained identical to those for the simulated aquaculture wastewater.

Nitrate (NO3 −) and Nitrite (NO2 −) concentrations were determined by ion chromatography (HIC-ESP Shimadzu, Japan). Concentrations of ammonium (NH4 +) were analyzed using Nessler's reagent colorimetric spectrophotometric method at 420 nm. Nitrous oxide (N2O) concentration was determined by gas chromatography (7890A Angilent, USA). The gas sampling procedure is outlined as follows: initially, helium gas (containing 20 % oxygen) is used to purge the headspace of the reactor to eliminate any nitrogen present before the experiment begins. Subsequently, the reactor is sealed with butadiene rubber. Throughout the experiment, gas samples are collected from the headspace using a syringe and then preserved in vacuum tubes (Labco, Exetainer, UK). After the sampling process, an equivalent volume of helium gas is reintroduced to the reactor to ensure the maintenance of pressure equilibrium.

Oxytetracycline concentration was tested by high-performance liquid chromatography (L-2000 Hitachi Japan) following a similar method as in our previous work [20]. Furthermore, oxytetracycline degradation intermediates were determined by ultra-performance liquid chromatography (UPLC-MS/MS) coupled with a tandem triple quadrupole mass spectrometer (ABI6500 Sciex, USA). The determining method- for Fathead minnow LC50 was based on Structure-Activity Relationships (QSAR) which is a computational simulation method that primarily relies on various molecular descriptors and model algorithms to establish the qualitative/quantitative relationships between a compound's structure and its physicochemical properties, biological activities, toxicological effects [22,23]. Sample pretreatment and test conditions of intermediates are provided in the supplementary information Method 2.

Photo-electrochemical characterizations were tested using a three-electrode configuration with an electrochemical workstation (CHI660D, CHI Instruments Inc., China). ITO, NCDS/g-C3N4/ITO, and bio/NCDS/g-C3N4/ITO were used as working electrodes, respectively. We used a Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode and a carbon rod as the counter electrode for all photo-electrochemical analyses. Amperometric I-t curves and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were analyzed without bias. Linear sweep voltammograms were performed from 0.05 to 0.80 V. During all photo-electrochemical tests 50 mM PBS was used as an electrolyte. A light intensity of 100 mW/cm2 was applied in photoelectrochemical testing. The electron transportation system activity (ETSA) was determined by reducing 2-(p-iodophenyl)-3-(p-nitrophenyl)-5-phenyl tetrazolium chloride (INT, a kind of exogenous electron acceptor) to formazan (INF). Detailed test processes are provided in the supplementary information Method 3.

To investigate the microbial community structure, carriers from biodegradation treatment and synergistic treatment were collected and immersed in 30 mL of 50 mM PBS, followed by ultrasound for 10 min to allow the microbial communities to settle in the PBS. The mixture was centrifuged at 5000 g/min, and the pellet at the bottom was collected and stored at − 20 °C. DNA was extracted using FastDNA SPIN Kit for Soil (MP Biomedicals, USA). The extracted DNA was sent to Majorbio Bio-pharm Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China) for amplicon sequencing. The PCR thermal program was set at 95 °C for 3.0 min. Then, 27 cycles were conducted at 95 °C for 30.0 s, 55 °C for 30.0 s, and 72 °C for 45.0 s, respectively. The final extension was conducted at 72 °C for 10 min. The selected bacterial primers were 338F (ACTCCTACGGGAGGCAGCAG) and 806R (GGACTACHVGGGTWTCTAAT). Compositions of the mixture for PCR reactions are shown in Table S5. Raw sequence data of samples has been recorded to the National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI) database with accession numbers of PRJNA1086845.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Integrated removal of nitrate and oxytetracycline
ce:section.ce:para: A synergistic system integrating photocatalysis and microbes was employed for the simultaneous removal of nitrate and oxytetracycline. For control experiments, different treatments including biodegradation, photocatalysis, absorption, and photolysis were conducted (Fig. 1 ).

As shown in Fig. 1a, photocatalysis, absorption, and photolysis exhibited negligible influence on nitrate reduction. Conversely, the biodegradation achieved a 32.4 % reduction in nitrate concentration within 24 h, while the synergistic degradation eliminated nitrate. The data obtained from the synergistic degradation treatment were fitted to a pseudo-first-order equation:
C =
C 0
e
kt
. The synergistic degradation in this study exhibited the rate constant (k) of 0.185 h−1(R2 = 0.976), representing the highest observed removal efficiency as we investigated (Table S6).

Oxytetracycline concentration was shown in Fig. 1b. The removal rates were ranked as follows: photocatalysis (100 %) > synergistic degradation (91.2 %) > biodegradation (61.7 %) > photolysis (53.0 %) > absorption (39.3 %). Despite the acclimation of the microbial community for over 10 months in an oxytetracycline-containing wastewater, the oxytetracycline removal by biodegradation within 24 h was only 63.7 %. This indicates that oxytetracycline exhibited recalcitrant biodegradability and had an inhibitory impact on microbial growth, and metabolism. However, the synergistic treatment attained a 91.2 % removal efficiency, highlighting the improvement in oxytetracycline degradation through photocatalytic-microbial synergy. The degradation efficiency of the synergistic treatment (91.2 %) was slightly lower than that of the photocatalytic treatment (100 %). This discrepancy may be attributed to the presence of microorganisms on the photocatalyst surface. Previous research stated that a biofilm with a thickness of 100 μm can block 88 % of light (with an intensity of 100 μmol/m2/s) [24]. It also has been shown that in mature biofilms, the attenuation of incident light intensity appears to be an inevitable occurrence [25]. Light intensity is an important factor that affects the efficiency of photocatalysts. Therefore, the reasons mentioned above explain why photocatalysis is more effective in removing oxytetracycline than synergistic treatment.

To gain insights into the nitrate denitrification process, the concentrations of potential intermediates involved in nitrate reduction, including nitrite, nitrous oxide, and ammonia were assessed (Fig. 2 ).

Fig. 2a illustrates the nitrite concentration. In the biodegradation treatment, 14.1 mg/L cumulative nitrite was observed at 24 h. However, in the synergistic treatment, a minor accumulation at 4 h, reaching the concentration of 0.8 mg/L, and then the nitrite underwent rapid transformation. This indicates the effectiveness of synergistic degradation in reducing nitrite accumulation. The cumulative nitrite concentration in photocatalytic treatment, adsorption treatment, and photolysis treatment consistently remained below the detection limit. This, in conjunction with the reduction of nitrate in Fig. 1a, suggests that the photocatalyst(NCDS/g-C3N4) poses challenges in directly reducing nitrate by photocatalysis.

The alteration in nitrous oxide (N2O) concentration was depicted in Fig. 2b. In the synergistic treatment, a 24-hour cumulative concentration of 42.4 µg/L was observed, which accounts for 0.06 % of the total converted nitrite. In the biodegradation treatment, 24.4 µg/L of N2O was accumulated within 24 h accounting for 0.22 % of the total converted nitrite. This indicates that N2O production in the synergistic treatment does not exhibit a significant increase in comparison to the biodegradation treatment.

In Fig. 2c, ammonia concentrations among different treatments were compared. The 24-hour removal rates ranked as follows: synergistic degradation (36.8 %) > biodegradation (35.6 %) > adsorption (9.1 %) > photolysis (4.6 %) > photocatalysis (2.2 %). The reduction of ammonia observed in both the biodegradation and synergistic degradation can be attributed to the presence of ammonia-oxidizing microorganisms, which are capable of oxidizing ammonia into nitrate. Furthermore, the assimilation of ammonia by microorganisms may also contribute to the decrease in ammonia levels.

The N2 production proportions were illustrated in Fig. 2d and were ranked as follows: synergistic degradation (82.6 %) > biodegradation (20.4 %) > adsorption (6.1 %) > photocatalysis (2.8 %) > photolysis (1.9 %). According to the calculation results, the predominant product of the synergistic treatment was N2.

To investigate the mechanism of oxytetracycline degradation, intermediates were identified using UPLC-MS/MS, as presented in Fig. 3 and Fig. S4. The presence of A (M/Z = 437) in all treatments signified that the degradation was initiated by a carbonyl-extrusion reaction at the C12 position. Due to the lower bond energy between C1-C12a in the oxytetracycline structure compared to that of C1-C2, it becomes more vulnerable to attack, resulting in the loss of the carbonyl group [26].

During the biodegradation process, A was produced from the oxytetracycline parent through decarboxylation [27], and then degraded by removing two methyl groups, leading to the formation of B (M/Z = 408). Additionally, the hydroxyl group of B at position C12a was eliminated in the formation of C (M/Z = 391). D (M/Z = 373) was generated followed by the replacement of two amino groups with a methyl group. Subsequently, the hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the heterocyclic ring were eliminated to produce E (M/Z = 325), which then underwent ring opening to ultimately yield F (M/Z = 253).

In the synergistic degradation, the oxytetracycline parent similarly underwent an initial decarboxylation step to produce A. Then G (M/Z = 413) formed by hydroxylation on CH3-N-CH3. Subsequently, G underwent dehydroxylation and deamination to yield H (M/Z = 353). Ultimately, a series of small molecular products, including I (M/Z = 219), K (M/Z = 157), and L (M/Z = 145), was generated by the cleavage of the benzene ring in polycyclic intermediates [28], and these products further mineralized to form CO2 and H2O. When comparing the intermediate products of synergistic degradation to those from biodegradation alone, the synergistic treatment primarily generates smaller, low molecular weight intermediates This indicates a more comprehensive degradation of intermediates within the synergistic system. In summary, the synergistic system is more efficient at breaking down the intermediates, underscoring the crucial role of photocatalysis in enhancing the microbial degradation of oxytetracycline intermediates within this synergistic system.

To better understand the photocatalysis process's role within the synergistic system, intermediates in photocatalysis were identified, as depicted in Fig. S4. Similar to the biodegradation and synergistic degradation, the photocatalysis treatment also generated intermediate A. The mass spectral peak areas of this intermediate were illustrated in Fig. S5 for three different treatments. At 6 h, the peak area of intermediate A in photocatalysis treatment is significantly higher than that of the synergistic system and biodegradation. This suggests that the photocatalysis treatment can convert oxytetracycline into intermediate A rapidly. To assess the toxicity of oxytetracycline and its degradation intermediates, we used the Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T., version 5.1.1.0) and applied quantitative structure–activity relationship (QSAR) methodology to predict key toxicity indicators, including Fathead minnow LC50 and developmental toxicity [29]. As revealed in Fig. 3b, the acute toxicity value of oxytetracycline for Fathead minnow LC50 is 1.21 mg/L, indicating significant toxicity. Following photocatalysis treatment, only two intermediates, M and Q, exhibited increased toxicity, while the rest of the compounds demonstrated reduced toxicity, indicating a general reduction in acute toxicity throughout the photocatalysis process. Similarly, most intermediates exhibited lower developmental toxicity (Fig. 3c), with the exceptions of intermediate M and S. Therefore, it indicates that within the synergistic system, the photocatalysis process rapidly decreases the inhibitory effect of oxytetracycline on microorganisms. This reduction in inhibitory effect is particularly beneficial for the thorough removal of oxytetracycline intermediate products by microorganisms.

Fig. S6a illustrates that the nitrate removal rate in the biodegradation treatment (without oxytetracycline addition) was 84.3 %, exceeding the removal rate (32.4 %) observed in biodegradation treatments (with oxytetracycline addition, depicted in Fig. 1a). The inhibited nitrate removal is possibly attributed to antibiotics’ toxicity to hinder denitrification enzyme activity, consequently reducing denitrification efficiency [30]. As shown in Fig. 1a, the nitrate removal efficiency under synergistic degradation treatment with oxytetracycline addition reached nearly 100 %, exceeding that of the biodegradation treatment without oxytetracycline addition (84.3 %) despite oxytetracycline inhibition. These results indicate that the synergistic degradation not only alleviates the inhibitory effects of oxytetracycline on denitrifying microorganisms but also further enhances their nitrate reduction capabilities. This enhancement may be attributed to the photogenerated electrons, which could modulate the metabolism of microorganisms, such as denitrification bacteria, thereby facilitating nitrate reduction.

As depicted in Fig. S6b, accumulated nitrite in the biodegradation treatment without oxytetracycline addition reached 37.1 mg/L at 24 h. This suggests that even without the inhibitory effect of oxytetracycline, the electrons supplied by the low carbon–nitrogen ratio of 2/1 may be insufficient to support complete biological denitrification, leading to substantial nitrite accumulation. On the contrary, synergistic treatment could convert nitrite completely (Fig. 2a). This observation reaffirms that photocatalysis not only reduces the microbial toxicity of oxytetracycline but also directly enhances microbial denitrification.

Photogenerated electron transfer between photocatalysts and microorganisms holds significance in combined pollution removal. The confirmation of interfacial electron transfer between NCDS/g-C3N4 and microorganisms was demonstrated through a photo-electrochemical assessment. Fig. 4 a shows the photocurrent response of the various treatments. The synergistic system (photocatalyst coupled microbe) exhibited the highest current density at 0.166 µA/cm2. This current density significantly exceeded that observed in the photocatalyst treatment (0.023 µA/cm2) and the blank treatment (0.005 µA/cm2). In the photocatalyst treatment, photocurrent was generated when NCDS/g-C3N4 was excited by visible light. Notably, the photocurrent swiftly diminished upon cessation of light, indicating the effective performance of NCDS/g-C3N4 as a photocatalyst. In the synergistic treatment, microorganisms facilitated the separation of photogenerated electrons generated on the NCDS/g-C3N4 surface, mitigating hole and photoelectron complexation, and consequently leading to a substantial enhancement in photocurrent. Specifically, it has been reported that microorganism membrane proteins were capable of capturing electrons generated on semiconductor surfaces [31]. In addition, oxidation-reducing substances in extracellular polymer substances, such as riboflavin [32–34] and pyocyanin [35,36], might also facilitate photoelectrons transfer to microorganisms.

In Fig. 4b, linear sweep voltammetry was conducted without light excitation. The synergistic treatment exhibited a notable increase in current in comparison to both photocatalyst and blank treatments. This phenomenon implies that the internal resistance within the synergistic treatment was smaller than that of the photocatalyst treatment. Subsequently, as depicted in Fig. 4c, the interfacial impedance within the synergistic system was markedly lower than that of the photocatalyst and blank treatments, thus further confirming the aforementioned findings. The reduced resistance observed at the photocatalyst-microorganisms interface suggests the presence of multiple electron transfer and metabolic pathways, allowing for more efficient utilization of photogenerated electrons.

The electron transport system activity (ETSA) test was conducted to elucidate the influence of photoelectrons on microorganisms metabolic processes (Fig. 4d). ETSA value of the synergistic treatment was 0.55 μg O2 g−1 protein min−1, which was higher than that of the biological treatment (0.39 μg O2 g−1 protein min−1). This indicates that the microorganism metabolism was boosted through photoelectron transfer. Enhanced ETSA can augment the efficiency of microorganisms to remove nitrate [37]. This phenomenon was reported in microorganism-TiO2/C3N4 photocatalysis synergistic system for denitrification [38]. In addition, increased ETSA has been found to bolster the degradation of oxytetracycline and its associated intermediates [15]. These findings suggest that the utilization of photogenerated electrons could enhance microorganism metabolism, consequently improving the biodegradation of nitrate-oxytetracycline pollutants.

Conclusively, the photocatalysis-microorganisms synergistic system is not merely a combination of two technologies. Rather, the photocatalysis process exerts a regulatory influence on the growth and metabolic processes of the microbial community, ultimately boosting the removal of combined pollutants.

Scanning electron microscopy was employed for the observation of blank carrier, carrier loaded with NCDS/g-C3N4, and the NCDS/g-C3N4 coated carrier with cultured biofilms, as illustrated in Fig. S4. The blank polyurethane fiber (Fig. S7a and b) exhibited a relatively flat and smooth surface morphology. In contrast, the carrier loaded with NCDS/g-C3N4 (Fig. S7c and d) underwent a notable color change, shifting from white to yellowish brown. Additionally, the carrier surfaces showed increased roughness, attributed to the presence of NCDS/g-C3N4 nanosheets adhering to the fiber structure. After cultivation, microorganisms were tightly bound to NCDS/g-C3N4 (Fig. S7e and S7f). This close interaction was crucial, as it enabled the efficient separation of photogenerated carriers generated by NCDS/g-C3N4 during photocatalysis and enhanced microorganism metabolic activities.

To examine the influence of photogenerated electrons on microbial communities we characterized the taxonomic and functional profiles of bacterial communities. The α-diversity indices for the microbial community between the biodegradation treatment and the synergistic treatment are detailed in Table S7. ACE and Chao 1 indices suggest that the richness of the microbial communities in both treatments was relatively similar. However, when considering the Simpson and Shannon indices, microbial diversity was significantly increased in the synergistic degradation treatment. This observation further underscores the role of photogenerated electrons in regulating the microbial community. These changes are conducive to improving the capacity of functional microorganisms in pollutant removal processes.

At the genus level, the bacterial community analyses revealed that genera known for their ability to facilitate extracellular electron transfer, such as Pseudomonas (Huang et al., 2011), Chryseobacterium [39], Alicycliphilus [40], Cloacibacterium [41], exhibited a higher relative abundance within the synergistic treatment compared to the degradation treatment, as depicted in Fig. 5 a. This observation was supported by microbial KEGG prediction, conducted through a Tax4Fun-based approach. Functions linked to electrons transportation, such as fcca related to cytochrome c on the outer membrane, ribA and menE associated with redox-active electron transfer shuttles, and piliA associated with nanowires, exhibited a substantial increase in the synergistic treatment (Fig. 5b). This upregulation confirms the microorganisms' potential for the transfer and metabolism of photogenerated electrons. Consequently, this enhanced electron transport process elucidated the higher photogenerated current observed in the synergistic treatment (Fig. 4a).

Specific genera, such as Nubsella, Chryseobacterium, Zoogloea, Methylobacterium, and Stenotrophomonas, exhibited a notable increase. They have previously been reported for their capacity to degrade aromatic organic compounds such as p-nitrophenol [42], toluene [43,44], and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [45] like phenanthrene [46], through benzene ring cleavage. The main structure of tetracycline consists of four benzene rings, indicating that microorganisms with the ability to degrade tetracyclines must inherently possess the mechanism to decompose aromatic rings. One important mechanism for microbial degradation of benzene rings is hydroxylation through monooxygenase [47], followed by further ring opening. This function is similar to that of the protein FAD-binding monooxygenase encoded by the tetracycline resistance gene tet(X). Therefore, microbes capable of degrading aromatic rings also have the potential to degrade antibiotics. The observed increase in these bacterial genera suggested that the photocatalytic process optimized the microbial community structure to enhance the degradation of oxytetracycline and its associated intermediates. In KEGG functional prediction, specific enzymatic functions associated with oxytetracycline degradation [48], such as EC: 1.3.1.74, EC: 3.5.2.14, EC: 3.5.4.12, EC: 1.6.3.3, EC: 1.14.13.8 were identified (Fig. 5d). Notably, enzymes involved in benzene ring hydroxylation EC: 3.5.2.14 and EC: 1.14.13.8 [49] and deamidation EC: 3.5.4.12 [50] exhibited significant up-regulation. This observation indicates an enhanced benzene ring deamination and hydroxylation, facilitating deep degradation of oxytetracycline and its associated intermediate within the synergetic degradation treatment.

Zoogloea [51], Mesorhizobium [52], Pseudomonas [53], Stenotrophomonas [54], Cloacibacterium [55] were recognized as typical autotrophic denitrifying microorganisms. The elevated abundance of these genera in synergetic treatment suggested that photogenerated electrons stimulated the growth of autotrophic denitrifying microorganisms. Specifically, these genera appear to utilize photogenerated electrons for denitrification. Simultaneously, an increase in the abundance of Methyloversatilis [56], Caulobacter [57], and Alicycliphilus [58] which are heterotrophic denitrifying microorganisms, was observed in the synergetic treatment. This increase may be attributed to the utilization of oxytetracycline degradation intermediates as organic carbon sources. FAPROTAX, a common tool for predicting functional annotations related to biogeochemical cycling processes, including elements like carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, and sulfur, was employed. Fig. 5c revealed significant upregulation in functions associated with nitrous oxide denitrification, nitrite respiration, and nitrate reduction in synergistic degradation. This indicates a substantial enhancement in microorganism denitrification under the synergistic treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous removal of nitrate and oxytetracycline in actual aquaculture wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: Aquaculture wastewater was collected to explore the system's removal efficiencies of nitrate and oxytetracycline, as shown Fig. 6 .

As observed in Fig. 6a, the nitrate removal efficiency in the biodegradation treatment after 24 h was 18.8 %, which was lower than the 32.4 % that was achieved in simulated wastewater. This diminished efficiency in actual wastewater may be attributed to the presence of large molecular soluble organics, such as humus, which pose challenges for heterotrophic denitrifying microbes to utilize [59], leading to slower microbial growth [60]. In contrast, the 24-h removal efficiency of the synergistic treatment was notably higher at 39.4 %, equating to 2.1 times that of the biodegradation treatment alone. This increase in denitrification efficiency can be attributed to the photocatalytic process, where the generated electrons are utilized by autotrophic denitrifying microbes for metabolism and nitrate reduction. This suggests that in actual aquaculture wastewater, even though the utilization of organic carbon sources by microbes is hindered, leading to the reduction in efficiency, the electrons supplied by photocatalysis in the synergisitc system effectively maintain autotrophic denitrification.

As depicted in Fig. 6b, the removal efficiencies of oxytetracycline are ranked in descending order among different treatments, photocatalytic (97.3 %), synergistic (75.8 %), biodegradation (41.9 %), photolysis (26.1 %), and adsorption (9.5 %). Similar to the trend observed in nitrate removal, the oxytetracycline removal efficiency of the biodegradation treatment (41.9 %) in actual wastewater is significantly lower compared to the efficiency observed in simulated wastewater (61.7 %). A contributing factor to this discrepancy could be the presence of trace elements, like iron and copper ions, in the simulated wastewater. These elements serve as active centers for many oxytetracycline oxidoreductases [49] and are crucial for microbial degradation. In contrast, the absence of these trace elements in actual wastewater may lead to a decrease in the relevant degrading enzymes, thereby reducing oxytetracycline degradation efficiency. It is noteworthy that the degradation efficiency of the synergistic treatment is 1.8 times higher than that of the biodegradation alone. This enhancement is attributed to the photocatalytic process, which generates holes and free radicals capable of initiating the inactivation and degradation of oxytetracycline. This not only reduces its toxic impact on microbes but also boosts the biodegradation efficiency of oxytetracycline and its intermediates. These findings indicate that despite the lower expression of oxytetracycline degrading enzymes in actual wastewater, the synergistic system is instrumental in achieving efficient oxytetracycline inactivation and degradation.

Despite the fact that in actual aquaculture wastewater, functional microbial groups exhibit varying degrees of reduced degradation efficiency for nitrate and oxytetracycline compared to simulated wastewater, the synergistic system still offers a significant advantage. The process of photocatalysis within this synergistic system markedly improves the degradation performance compared to single biodegradation treatments. This enhancement underscores the potential of integrating photocatalysis into traditional biodegradation methods to effectively treat actual aquaculture wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the synergistic system on enhancing oxytetracycline and nitrate simultaneous degradation
ce:section.ce:para: We proposed a potential mechanism for a synergistic system aiming to address nitrate-oxytetracycline pollution and achieve the removal of their intermediates (Fig. 7 ).

In low C/N ratio wastewater, the growth and metabolism of microorganisms were significantly hindered due to the lack of carbon sources. Additionally, the presence of oxytetracycline can be toxic to these microorganisms, further impeding their ability to remove nitrate and oxytetracycline. In the synergistic system, enhanced microbial degradation of nitrate-oxytetracycline can be delineated into two phases. In the initial stages, electron excitation leads to their transition to the conduction band and generates hole-electron pairs when exposed to visible light excitation. Concurrently, the photogenerated holes hold a pivotal role in oxytetracycline oxidation degradation by mediating processes such as decarbonylation, demethylation, and hydroxylation, leading to the formation of tetracyclic and tricyclic intermediates as products. Notably, as a direct consequence of the structural degradation of oxytetracycline, the toxicity of tetracyclic and tricyclic intermediates to microorganisms is relatively low, thereby fostering microorganism proliferation and enhancing microbial community diversity.

Subsequently, the photoelectrons are efficiently transported through the biofilms, leading to an augmentation in microbial metabolic activity. This increased microbial activity translates into an enhanced capacity for the removal of nitrate and oxytetracycline. Specifically, up-regulation of denitrification-related functions was observed, including processes such as nitrate reduction, nitrite respiration, and nitrous oxide denitrification. Simultaneously, the degradation associated with oxytetracycline and its intermediates, including but not limited to benzene ring hydroxylation and deamidation, experiences a notable boost. Hence, this enhanced microbial function contributes to the efficient removal of nitrate-oxytetracycline pollutants.

This study presents a synergistic system, aiming to enhance the simultaneous degradation of nitrate and oxytetracycline. In simulated wastewater environment, nitrate was effectively eliminated, and a substantial 91.2 % removal of oxytetracycline was achieved within 24 h. Notably, the enhanced biofilm exhibited an exceptional ability to comprehensively remove intermediates of the combined pollutants. The synergistic system not only mitigates oxytetracycline inhibition by rapidly degrading its structure but also enhances the microorganism metabolism by supplying photogenerated electrons. Furthermore, when tested under actual aquaculture wastewater, the synergistic system demonstrated a substantial enhancement in both nitrate and oxytetracycline biodegradation efficiency. This research provides a prospective strategy to enhance microbial degradation of nitrate-oxytetracycline combined pollution in aquaculture wastewater with low C/N ratios.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Declaration of Generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
ce:section.ce:para: During the preparation of this work, the authors used Chatgpt to improve English writing. After using this tool, the authors reviewed and edited the content as needed and took full responsibility for the content of the publication.
